title: The Demographic Dead End: 2026 State of Fertility Report | Institute for Family Studies
gist: IFS's inaugural State of Fertility report presents new historical fertility data back to 1660, argues US fertility (now below 1.6) has entered its deepest and longest below-replacement episode, projects population peaking and declining as early as the 2050s, and prescribes large pronatal cash policies plus cultural interventions on friendship and celebrity to lift birth rates toward 2.4.

insights.claim: US total fertility is now below 1.6 children per woman, and the country is in its third historic episode of extended below-replacement fertility — longer, lower and more geographically widespread than the 1933-41 and 1972-89 declines.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Frames the current fertility slump as structurally worse than prior dips, not a cyclical blip that will self-correct.
insights.locator: Major Findings

insights.claim: The gap between actual US fertility (below 1.6) and the roughly 2.4 children Americans say they want is the widest since the early 1970s and approaching Great Depression levels, meaning fertility is falling further below stated desires.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: The desire-reality gap implies latent unmet demand for family formation that policy could theoretically unlock.
insights.locator: Desired family size has not declined

insights.claim: The correlation between a cohort's completed fertility and the total fertility rate prevailing when that cohort turned 25 is 0.96, making current low TFRs a near-deterministic predictor that completed family sizes will fall to new lows.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: A predictive relationship: today's low period rates lock in low completed fertility for a generation, hardening the demographic outlook.
insights.quote: The correlation between actual births per woman near the end of a cohort’s reproductive years and the total fertility rate prevailing when that cohort turned 25 is 0.96
insights.locator: As can be seen, while completed fertility

insights.claim: Across US history births have outnumbered immigrants roughly 4 to 1 (about 550 million born vs 140 million immigrants since 1776), so native fertility, not migration, has always been the primary driver of American population growth.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Reframes the growth engine as domestic fertility, undercutting reliance on immigration as a substitute.
insights.locator: Why did America undershoot expectations?

insights.claim: Massachusetts' birth rate is now around 1.35 children per woman, one of the lowest in the nation and on par with Europe, and its cohort aged 40 in 2025 has completed fertility of just 1.6 — lower than in over 200 of 237 countries and comparable to post-Soviet Eastern Europe.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Wealthy US regions already have collapsed-fertility profiles matching demographic-crisis countries, signaling where labor-supply strain hits first.
insights.locator: For women who are in their early 40s today

insights.claim: In 2025 only South Dakota still has fertility near population-stability level, while every other state is below replacement, with the West Coast, New England, Illinois and Florida very low.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Sub-replacement fertility is now nationwide, removing the assumption that high-fertility regions offset low ones.
insights.locator: Figure 10. Total fertility rate by state, 2025

insights.claim: California, once not a low-fertility state, fell to just 1.42 children per woman in 2025, and Nevada, Arizona and New Mexico have also seen fertility plunge as Hispanic fertility rates dropped rapidly.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: The collapse of Hispanic fertility removes a demographic buffer many long-run US labor and consumption forecasts assumed.
insights.locator: Thus, whereas California was not a low fertility state

insights.claim: North Dakota bucked the national fertility decline, presumably because the fracking boom fueled high incomes, high male employment and ultimately higher marriage rates.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Links male employment and income shocks (energy booms) causally to marriage and fertility — a channel to watch in resource regions.
insights.locator: North Dakota has famously seen its fertility rates

insights.claim: New York state had below-replacement fertility in 1929 before the stock market crash, indicating the Great Depression intensified but did not cause the US fertility decline that was already underway from 1917.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Cautions against blaming fertility declines purely on economic shocks; the secular trend predates them.
insights.locator: The District of Columbia has almost always

themes.theme: Friendship and peer culture as fertility drivers

insights.claim: Having very helpful versus relatively unsupportive friends is associated with nearly a full additional child in desired family size for young Americans (around 1.7 vs 2.8) and raises couple intentions to have another child by about 10 percentage points.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Identifies supportive social networks as a large, underused lever on fertility separate from money.
insights.locator: For Americans under 30 with the bottom 10%

insights.claim: The association between helpful friends and fertility is similar to or larger than the effect of religious attendance, and remains after controlling for religion, so it is not merely that religious people have more helpful friends.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Friendship quality rivals religiosity as a fertility predictor, broadening the set of actionable cultural interventions.
insights.locator: But the associational effect of helpful friends

insights.claim: The average family size of a person's peers has twice as much effect on their family-size desires as their own childhood number of siblings, and number of cousins shows no association at all.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Fertility desire is shaped more by current peer culture than family-of-origin, so peer-network interventions matter more than heredity of family size.
insights.quote: fertility desires are more about peer culture than family culture
insights.locator: Other questions we asked about parent-child

insights.claim: Each additional child born to an admired public figure predicts higher desired family size (up to about 0.15 children per extra celebrity child), an effect about as large as an individual's own number of siblings and stronger for women.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Quantifies celebrity influence on fertility desire, supporting celebrity-enlistment as a pronatal strategy, especially targeting women.
insights.locator: Another topic we surveyed

insights.claim: Passively having friends with kids or admiring a celebrity with kids shapes aspirations but does not predict actual intentions to have more children — only genuinely helpful, supportive peers move behavioral intentions.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Distinguishes aspiration from action: awareness campaigns raise desire, but concrete support is what converts desire into births.
insights.quote: prospective parents need peers who are genuinely helpful and supportive, not just those who demonstrate big families
insights.locator: To shape behavioral intentions

insights.claim: The report argues the digital revolution — smartphones, social media and pornography replacing in-person social life — plausibly reduces fertility by cutting people's exposure to family life.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Ties declining birth rates to screen-mediated social withdrawal, implicating technology platforms in the demographic story.
insights.locator: Moreover, given the apparently large role

insights.claim: If fertility continues its recent decline US population will peak around 351 million and begin declining in the 2050s; if fertility merely stabilizes, it peaks around 366 million and declines in the 2080s.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Puts a date range on US population peak — a structural headwind for domestic demand, housing and labor within many investors' horizons.
insights.locator: U.S. population growth has abruptly slowed

insights.claim: The report argues official forecasters (UN, CBO, Census, Social Security and Medicare Trustees) are too optimistic because they assume flat future net migration and an unjustified fertility recovery.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: If mainstream demographic assumptions are too rosy, entitlement solvency and long-run growth models are systematically overstated.
insights.locator: To start with, it must be noted

insights.claim: Net migration has already fallen to near-zero under President Trump and the report assumes immigration cannot remain permanently high because global fertility decline will leave fewer young emigrants and more countries competing for them.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Undercuts immigration as a reliable long-run population backstop — a bearish input for US labor-force growth assumptions.
insights.locator: First, they tend to adopt a very standard

insights.claim: The base scenario used for Social Security, Medicare and Medicaid budget planning assumes a rapid fertility increase similar to the report's optimistic 'Revival' case, which the authors regard as unrealistic.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Implies entitlement trust-fund projections rest on an implausible fertility rebound, biasing solvency estimates optimistic.
insights.locator: The Social Security Trust Fund (OASDI)

insights.claim: The flagship proposal, 'American Birthday Accounts,' would invest about $15,000 per US-born child, accessible only when the beneficiary has their own child, potentially maturing into a ~$100,000 baby bonus for a first birth.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: A concrete large-scale cash-transfer design that, if enacted, would channel tens of billions annually into family formation.
insights.locator: Reform 1: American Birthday Accounts

insights.claim: The authors estimate American Birthday Accounts would cost $45-80 billion per year (less than 1% of the federal budget, cheaper than proposed childcare or leave expansions) and could become self-funding as unclaimed funds recycle into a rolling national family trust.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Positions a six-figure baby bonus as fiscally modest, changing the political feasibility calculus for large pronatal transfers.
insights.locator: Bang-for-buck, American Birthday Accounts

insights.claim: Based on responses to cash incentives studied abroad, the report estimates American Birthday Accounts would boost fertility 20-40%, which if applied to today's reproductive-age Americans would lift birth rates to 1.9-2.2 children per woman — near replacement.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: An aggressive elasticity estimate; if even partly right, cash policy alone could close much of the fertility gap.
insights.locator: Assuming American fertility responds to cash incentives

insights.claim: The report cites that Sweden, Canada and the UK all saw fertility rise after switching from joint to individualized tax filing, associated with about 0.05-0.15 more children per woman, and urges eliminating US marriage penalties in Medicaid, SNAP, Section 8, EITC and other programs.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Identifies means-tested-program marriage penalties as a fixable structural drag on family formation.
insights.locator: Reform 3: End Marriage Penalties

insights.claim: There has been zero increase in US residential square footage per capita in 25 years and new construction is shifting to small apartments, so the report proposes federal funding allocated per bedroom of housing completed.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Ties the housing-supply shortfall directly to family formation, a bullish signal for family-sized housing if pronatal housing policy advances.
insights.quote: There has been zero increase in residential square footage per capita in 25 years
insights.locator: Reform 4: Build Family Housing Now

insights.claim: The authors claim their combined five reforms could raise US birth rates to at least 2.1 and perhaps 2.4 children per woman.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Sets the policy ambition and benchmark against which any enacted pronatal package would be judged.
insights.locator: In combination, we believe instituting these reforms

insights.claim: The report provocatively suggests paying Taylor Swift a billion dollars to have children might produce more births than spending the same money on child tax credits if it sways her fanbase, and cites France's family-quotient tax breaks as a way to stimulate elite fertility with downstream cultural spillovers.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Illustrates the thesis that elite and celebrity fertility has outsized cultural leverage — a rationale for high-income pronatal tax breaks.
insights.quote: paying Taylor Swift a billion dollars to have children might produce more children in society than spending the same money on child tax credits
insights.locator: Norm 1: Enlist Celebrity Culture

insights.claim: Under-30s with fewer than 4 close friends average around 0.7 children per friend, while those with 5 or more average 1-1.3, and the report proposes reviving in-person socializing via device restrictions in schools, Blue Laws and even 'Digital Blue Laws' taxing Sunday data or digital-ad usage.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Links social-network size to fertility and floats taxing digital services to nudge people offline — a novel policy risk vector for platforms.
insights.locator: Norm 3: Promote Real-Life Socializing

insights.claim: The report proposes legally recognized 'civil godparents,' safe-harbor and free-range-parenting liability protections, and status campaigns for 'care donation' (watching friends' kids) to make supportive friendship easier.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Sketches the legal and cultural scaffolding of a pronatal 'friendship agenda' distinct from cash transfers.
insights.locator: Norm 2: Encourage Supportive Friendship
